title: Speech and Occupational Therapy: What They Actually Do
summary: These two therapy types get mentioned constantly in recovery plans, but what they actually involve is often unclear to families.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Speech and Occupational Therapy: What They Actually Do | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: These two therapy types get mentioned constantly in recovery plans, but what they actually involve is often unclear to families.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Speech therapy covers far more than speaking — including swallowing and cognitive skills.
Occupational therapy focuses on the practical tasks of daily living.
Both often continue at home between formal sessions.

paragraphs: Speech-language pathologists work on far more than just talking — they also address swallowing difficulties (a real safety concern after stroke), and cognitive-communication skills like following conversations or organizing thoughts.

Occupational therapists focus on the practical mechanics of daily life — dressing, bathing, cooking, and using tools like buttons or utensils — often recommending specific adaptive equipment to make these tasks more manageable.

nextSteps: Ask each therapist what specific goals they're working toward.
Learn a few take-home exercises to reinforce between sessions.
Ask about adaptive equipment recommendations from occupational therapy.
disclaimer: General overview — each therapist can explain their specific plan and goals for your situation.
